user_query:
I'm a California-based tax consultant and want a quick, defensible security posture check on the tax-related sites my clients use or interact with. Please build and run a small script or command-line workflow that reads the domains from input/vendor_domains.csv and does the following for each domain:

1) Retrieve the security contact file defined by RFC 9116 from the official public site using the standard paths: first try /.well-known/security.txt (preferred), and if that is not available, try /security.txt. Also attempt to retrieve /robots.txt. These resources should be fetched over HTTPS on the listed domain.

2) Save any successfully fetched files under output/downloads/<domain>/ with the exact filenames security.txt and robots.txt as applicable. If a file is not found, do not create a placeholder file, but do record the attempt.

3) For each domain, write output/downloads/<domain>/meta.json that captures the HTTP attempts and results with keys: domain, attempted_paths (list of the two security.txt paths attempted in order), security_txt_status (map of path to HTTP status or error), robots_txt_status (HTTP status or error), chosen_security_txt_path (null if none), and fetched_at_utc (ISO timestamp).

4) Parse any retrieved security.txt to extract the following fields into a consolidated report: contacts (all values from lines beginning with "Contact:"), policy_expires (value from an "Expires:" line if present), acknowledgments (value from an "Acknowledgments:" line if present). For robots.txt, include robots_txt_present (boolean) and the number of Disallow lines (robots_disallow_count).

5) Produce two consolidated reports covering all domains listed in the CSV: output/report/security_posture.json and output/report/security_posture.csv. Each record/row must include exactly these fields: domain, org_name, used_by_clients (true/false), security_txt_present (true/false), security_txt_path ("/.well-known/security.txt", "/security.txt", or null), contacts (array in JSON; semicolon-separated in CSV), policy_expires (string or blank), acknowledgments (string or blank), robots_txt_present (true/false), robots_disallow_count (integer), last_checked_utc (ISO timestamp), and risk_rating with the following deterministic rule: Low if security_txt_present is true; Medium if security_txt_present is false but robots_txt_present is true; High if both are false.

Notes and constraints:
- Do not hardcode the domain list; use input/vendor_domains.csv as the single source of domains and organization names.
- The Internet retrieval is required; do not mock responses.
- Ensure all output paths are created under the output/ directory as specified.
- Use a repeatable script or command-line approach so I can re-run this later.

Deliverables I should see after you run it: the raw downloaded files under output/downloads/<domain>/, the per-domain output/downloads/<domain>/meta.json, and the consolidated output/report/security_posture.json and output/report/security_posture.csv. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
